company.name: Tesla

marketCap.rationale: Computed from Tesla's $17.00 IPO price and 93,109,393 shares outstanding after the offering and concurrent private placement in the final prospectus.

company.description: EV, charging, energy storage, and autonomy company that sells both products and future narratives.

overview.title: A car company, an energy company, and occasionally a mood board
paragraphs: Tesla deserves credit for forcing the auto industry to move faster. It also benefits from investors repeatedly pricing future software leverage into a business that still has to weld metal and navigate regulators.

That mix keeps Tesla in the middle of the decentralizability spectrum. Open charging, home energy management, and driver-assistance tooling can nibble at the software/control layer. Car manufacturing remains stubbornly physical and regulated.

moatNarrative: Tesla's moat rests on brand, battery and manufacturing iteration speed, charging infrastructure, and the promise of software differentiation.
Some of that moat is hard industrial reality. Some of it is narrative premium, which is not the same thing but does occasionally pay just as well.
decentralizationNarrative: Charging standards, energy management, and autonomy-adjacent tooling are much more openable than vehicle manufacturing itself.
Distributed energy tech also matters here: if household and community energy stacks become more modular, Tesla's control surface may narrow.
company.snapshotNote: Curated early-2026 public-source snapshot. Exact ordering can drift with the tape.

moat.rationale: Tesla has brand and infrastructure advantages, but much of the category remains contestable.

decentralizability.rationale: Charging and energy layers are openable, while vehicle manufacturing remains centralized.

profitability.rationale: Tesla is profitable but less predictably so than the platform-heavy names above it.

peRatio.rationale: Approximate valuation snapshot reflecting very high future expectations.

marketCap.rationale: Approximate market cap snapshot from public market trackers.

products.name: Supercharger network
products.summary: Tesla's branded fast-charging network and related ecosystem control surface.
products.whyItMatters: Charging infrastructure deepens customer lock-in and brand trust.
replacementSketch: Open charging standards and independently operated networks can erode brand-tied infrastructure advantage.
Localized energy production makes charging less dependent on a vertically integrated stack.

alternatives.name: OpenEVSE
alternatives.summary: Open EV charging hardware and control stack.

openness.rationale: A genuinely open answer to proprietary charging stacks.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Charging can be deployed locally without a single network landlord.

readiness.rationale: Strong for DIY and semi-professional deployments, less turnkey than Tesla's network.

costLeverage.rationale: Useful where openness matters more than brand convenience.

alternatives.name: OCPP-based charging ecosystem
alternatives.summary: Open Charge Point Protocol ecosystem for interoperable EV charging infrastructure.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Interoperability allows more operators to participate.

readiness.rationale: Already meaningful in real charging deployments.

costLeverage.rationale: Undercuts the assumption that fast charging must live inside one branded network.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open EV Charging Federation
disruptionConcepts.summary: A charging network where stations, payment rails, availability data, and loyalty layers are interoperable instead of tied to one branded network.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Break charging lock-in by making roaming, billing, and uptime competition portable across many station operators.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Lightning enables instant roaming settlement between drivers, station owners, and software operators with low overhead.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Independent chargers publish availability and pricing while routing apps and fleet services compose them into user-facing experiences.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Signed charger status, metered energy proofs, and random uptime audits reduce fake availability and billing disputes.
failureModes: Hardware reliability still matters more than protocol elegance
adoptionPath: Start with fleets and regional roaming partners
Expand into mainstream driver routing once uptime reporting is consistently trusted

decentralizationFit.rationale: This concept meaningfully shifts control away from a single incumbent operator.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The participant and incentive model is plausible but still operationally demanding.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Current tools and market structure could support an initial version without waiting for a full paradigm shift.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If adopted, the concept would chip away at pricing power or default distribution leverage.

disruptionConcepts.name: Community Energy Stop Kits
disruptionConcepts.summary: Local businesses, municipalities, and property owners assemble charging stops from open chargers, local storage, and solar canopies instead of depending on a single branded network footprint.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Unlike the first concept's charging federation, this one attacks the site-build and equipment stack that creates network concentration in the first place.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Open energy hardware makes more of the charging stop buildout modular and locally sourceable.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Site hosts combine open charger kits, local power assets, and service contracts while federated software still keeps discovery interoperable.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Charger uptime logs, payment receipts, and site power telemetry prove whether a stop is actually usable and economic.
failureModes: Permitting and grid interconnection still slow deployment
Drivers may still prefer the brand they already trust on long trips
adoptionPath: Start with destination charging and municipal sites
Expand to corridor charging as reliability data compounds

decentralizationFit.rationale: This concept decentralizes charging-stop hardware and local power supply into many site owners instead of one branded network builder.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The coordination loop is credible because site hosts can combine open chargers, storage, and software while still sharing discovery metadata.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Most primitives already exist; the equipment exists now, though interconnection and service consistency still need work.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If it scales, it pressures Tesla's advantage in proprietary charging-site deployment and bundled energy hardware.

products.name: Powerwall and Megapack
products.summary: Energy storage products and software for homes and grids.
products.whyItMatters: This is where Tesla's software and hardware story merges with the broader energy transition.
replacementSketch: Open energy management layers can reduce how much value one vendor captures from the full stack.
Printable solar, better local storage economics, and microgrid tooling could make the category much more modular.

alternatives.name: OpenEMS
alternatives.summary: Open energy management platform for distributed energy systems.

openness.rationale: Open control plane for energy orchestration.

readiness.rationale: Good technical base, still less consumer-friendly than integrated vendor stacks.

costLeverage.rationale: Useful as energy hardware becomes more modular and software-defined.

alternatives.name: Home Assistant Energy
alternatives.summary: Open home automation platform with growing energy monitoring and control capabilities.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Local-first automation fits distributed energy well.

readiness.rationale: Already widely used by technically comfortable households.

costLeverage.rationale: Reduces dependence on vertically integrated smart-home energy stacks.

disruptionConcepts.name: Household Energy Swarm
disruptionConcepts.summary: A network where homes, batteries, installers, and grid-service operators coordinate through open software rather than one vertically integrated battery brand.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Move energy storage value toward open coordination and installer ecosystems instead of a single brand stack.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Lightning can settle grid services and peer energy transactions while open protocols let hardware brands compete on equal terms.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Installers, homeowners, and local grid-service operators mix hardware and software under shared dispatch rules.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Metered dispatch logs, inverter attestations, and settlement records make delivered flexibility auditable.
failureModes: Policy and utility rules still shape the market heavily
adoptionPath: Start where virtual power plants already make sense
Broaden as open inverter and battery control standards improve

decentralizationFit.rationale: This concept meaningfully shifts control away from a single incumbent operator.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The participant and incentive model is plausible but still operationally demanding.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Current tools and market structure could support an initial version without waiting for a full paradigm shift.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If adopted, the concept would chip away at pricing power or default distribution leverage.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open Solar Appliance Microfactory
disruptionConcepts.summary: Open solar, storage, inverter, and small-wind modules become configurable home energy appliances that households or local shops can assemble, upgrade, and repair instead of buying a fixed vendor stack.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Unlike the first concept's swarm coordination, this one attacks Tesla's hardware bundle by making more of the home energy system locally buildable.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Printable solar research, open power electronics, and small wind hardware widen the range of components that can be assembled outside a closed vendor channel.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Design maintainers, local assemblers, and installers share validated hardware recipes while households buy interoperable modules over time.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Output telemetry, safety tests, and commissioning checklists keep locally assembled systems tied to measurable performance.
failureModes: Too much of the stack is still technically or regulatorily hard for household assembly
Warranty and safety expectations could keep buyers in closed systems
adoptionPath: Start with controllers, racks, and replaceable power modules
Move deeper into generation hardware only as the open stack proves itself

decentralizationFit.rationale: This concept decentralizes home energy hardware into interoperable locally assembled modules instead of one closed vendor stack.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The coordination loop is credible because validated hardware recipes let many assemblers and installers serve the same modular system.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The enabling pieces exist, but controllers and power modules are real today, but truly local generation hardware is still early.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If it scales, it pressures Tesla Energy's bundled hardware margins and ecosystem lock-in.
